fuzzy_prd: We're building the reporting layer for a dead-code scanner tool. The scanner already produces raw lists of problems — imports that couldn't be resolved, declared dependencies that are never actually used, and source files that nothing ever imports — and a separate list of what was cleaned up. We need a library that turns these raw results into consistent, human-readable terminal reports so every part of the tool renders results the same way. There are three things the reporting layer needs to do: first, a small helper that normalizes any value into a list (so code that accepts either a single item or a list can always work with a list uniformly); second, a scan results report that shows a summary header with the version and entry point information and then a boxed table for each non-empty problem category with numbered entries; and third, a cleanup report that shows which dependencies were removed and which files were deleted, using boxed tables for non-empty groups and a confirmation line when a group is empty. When the scan finds no problems the report is just a single confirmation line. All reports must fit within an 80-column terminal width and must not use any terminal color codes — handle the layout the same way the existing table-drawing utility does. The box-drawing characters and exact spacing must match the reference implementation exactly so outputs can be diffed.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: function formatList(caption: string, records: string[]): string; function formatMetaTable(caption: string, data: {...}, context: Context): string; function printDeleteResult({removedDeps, deletedFiles}: DeleteResult): void

trigger_keywords: what is the exact terminal width for the report
how wide are the report tables
what column count is used for layout
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All reports are laid out to exactly 80 columns total width. The gutter (index column) is 5 characters wide, followed by ' │ ', giving a content column of 80 - 5 - 2 = 73 characters.

trigger_keywords: what unicode characters are the table borders made of
what are the exact table border characters
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Table borders use Unicode box-drawing characters: '─' (U+2500) for horizontal lines, '┬' (U+252C) for top T-junction, '┼' (U+253C) for middle cross, '┴' (U+2534) for bottom T-junction, and '│' (U+2502) for the vertical separator between gutter and content. The full-width divider in the summary header uses '─' repeated 80 times.

trigger_keywords: what happens when both cleanup lists are empty
what is the message when removedDeps and deletedFiles are both empty
what single line is printed for a fully clean cleanup
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When both removedDeps and deletedFiles are empty, the cleanup report is the single line: '✓ There are no unused files or dependencies.' (no surrounding blank lines, no tables).

trigger_keywords: what happens when only one cleanup list is empty
what confirmation line appears when removedDeps is empty but deletedFiles is not
what message when deletedFiles is empty but deps were removed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When removedDeps is empty but deletedFiles is not: prints '✓ There are no unused dependencies.' then the files table. When deletedFiles is empty but removedDeps is not: prints '✓ There are no unused files.' then the deps table.

trigger_keywords: what is the entry file label format with multiple entry files
how are multiple entry files labeled in the summary
what label is used for entry file rows when there is more than one
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: With a single entry file the label is 'entry file' (no number). With multiple entry files the labels are 'entry file 1', 'entry file 2', etc. (with a trailing space before the number). When an entry file has an optional label, it is appended as ' — <label>' after the file path.

trigger_keywords: what is the hint line at the end of the scan report
what text appears at the end of a non-clean scan
what closing line follows the tables in the scan report
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The scan report ends with a blank line followed by: '       Inspect the results and run npx unimported -u to update ignore lists' (7 spaces indent, no trailing newline).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle the layout the same way the existing table-drawing utility does
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The formatList function in src/print.ts draws the boxed tables; the gutter width dynamically adjusts based on the number of records (Math.max(4, digits+1)), ensuring the index column is wide enough for the row count.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that detail. Please refer to the start.md specification and the public test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for authoritative requirements.

feature1_normalize_to_array.json.description: Normalize an arbitrary value into a list. If the value is already a list it is returned unchanged; if it is a scalar or object it is wrapped into a one-element list.

feature2_scan_summary.json.description: Render the result of a dead-code scan as a human-readable report. Clean scan yields single confirmation line. Non-clean scan yields summary header and boxed tables per non-empty category.

cases.expected_output: ✓ There don't seem to be any unimported files.

feature3_removal_summary.json.description: Render the outcome of a cleanup operation. Non-empty groups get boxed tables; empty groups get confirmation lines; both empty gives combined confirmation.

cases.expected_output: ✓ There are no unused files or dependencies.
